opp.ais_why:
• The Merlin product line is empty and Products does not include Merlin, Agentic AI or ANA - only iContract, iSource, iSupplier, eProcurement and eInvoicing. The description states bluntly: "informal process - no RFP - demos and decision. No strong compelling event". The buyer has not made AI a selection criterion.
• The May 6 2026 discovery call (31 minutes) with Klimack, Harris and Pacheco captured pain points centered on convoluted SAP processes, SAP SRM end-of-life, ease of use, EDI catalog integration and three-way invoice match. Must-haves: invoicing, e-catalogs and SRM replacement. None of the buyer quotes reference AI, Merlin, agentic or autonomous capability.
• AI importance is rated 'Important', but that is a Zycus-side field with no supporting buyer evidence. The actual product mix sold and the pain points discussed contain zero AI signals. Sourcing and CLM are only "nice-to-have" per the buyer - the narrow P2P scope further dampens any AI surface. This is Resistant.

sources: Avoma discovery 06 May 2026 (Joe Fischeco, Steven Klimack)
metrics.narrative: Pain is well articulated but unquantified. Joe Fischeco (06 May) named five must-have/nice-to-have needs centered on replacing end-of-life SAP SRM, high-volume invoice 3-way matching, e-catalog buying and spend analytics; Steven Klimack stressed end-user ease of use ('our SAP structure ... can be kinda convoluted'). Karson shared a public-data ROI document on 29 Jun and SABIC leadership has requested a supporting case study, but no SABIC-specific numbers (invoice volumes, cycle times, headcount, savings) are captured. With cost an explicit ranking criterion, the move is to collect SABIC's actual procurement data and build a SABIC-data ROI model to take to the budget owner before the top-5 to top-2 down-select.

sources: Avoma technical deep dive 10 Jun 2026 (Jacco de Lauder)
champion.narrative: Joe Fischeco (procurement co-lead, ABS) is the champion-in-development: he convenes the buying group, sets the evaluation process and engages on every commercial thread. But his advocacy is still process-level, not sponsorship — he has not introduced Zycus to the budget owner or lobbied for us against rivals. Steven Klimack and Jacco de Lauder are strong technical validators without commercial authority. The single thing that converts Joe from coordinator to champion is asking him to broker the EB meeting and arm him with a SABIC-data business case he can carry up. The move is to test and deepen Joe's sponsorship by requesting that introduction.

competition.narrative: A competitive Source-to-Pay displacement, not sole-sourced. Incumbents being replaced: SAP SRM (end-of-life) plus SAP Ariba in Europe ('they're gonna stop using it'). SABIC also runs a third-party sourcing orchestration pilot whose gap is no invoicing integration — Zycus's single-platform wedge. Critically, Joe described a formal top-5 narrowing to top-2 but has NOT disclosed the shortlist, so the live direct P2P peers are unknown. No specific named rival has surfaced, so none is asserted. The move is to ask Joe directly who else is in the top five so positioning and pricing can be tailored before the down-select.

sources: Avoma discovery 06 May 2026 (Steven Klimack, Joe Fischeco)
identify_pain.narrative: Pain is concrete and owner-attributed. Steven Klimack (06 May): SAP SRM is 'more or less end of life ... we're looking for something to replace SRM' and the setup 'can be kinda convoluted ... we're looking to make it as simple, straightforward for the end user.' Joe named high-volume invoice 3-way matching as a must-have ('we don't wanna go to a manual invoicing process'), loss of REBA for supplier onboarding, and a complex PR release strategy. The SAP SRM end-of-life clock is the compelling event, though no hard date was given.

paper_process.narrative: Contracting mechanics partially visible. On the CLM demo 17 Jun SABIC confirmed DocuSign for eSignatures and a homegrown/SharePoint contract repository, and referenced legal manager 'Kathleen' returning Monday, with Brittany Somerville and Brandon Lacey newly engaged. The 10 Jun session covered InfoSec posture (ISO 27001, SOC 1 & SOC 2 Type 2, GDPR-ready, US primary + DR). No MSA/SOW, redline process, or procurement channel confirmed. With a Nov close, the move is to name a paper owner and map the legal/procurement signature path now, so a commercial 'yes' does not stall before redlining.

economic_buyer.narrative: No economic buyer has appeared on any of the eight touchpoints. Joe Fischeco drives the evaluation but on 06 May described 'a combined effort ... people at my same role in Europe ... will be involved' — a committee above him Zycus has not met. Budgetary pricing was shared ~11 Jun and a public-data ROI on 29 Jun, yet there is no CFO/VP-Procurement meeting and no written approval path. For a $518k carve-out P2P decision this is the single largest risk and the reason Best Case is indefensible. The move is to have Joe name and broker the introduction to the Americas budget owner now — not to cold-outreach an unnamed EB, but to use the champion who authored the process as the path to power.

decision_process.narrative: Read from the call sequence: discovery (06, 15 May) → tailored demo (21 May) → SAP integration/InfoSec deep dive (10 Jun) → CLM demo (17 Jun), with a live SAP integration demo still to schedule. Joe (06 May) defined a top-5 ranking then top-2 deep review before a combined US/Europe decision. The thread has broadened from procurement to IT/ERP to legal. What is missing is the approval chain above Joe and any dated vendor-selection deadline. The move is to co-author a written mutual evaluation timeline with Joe that names the selection date and the EB sign-off step.

sources: Avoma discovery 06 May 2026 (Joe Fischeco, Steven Klimack)
decision_criteria.narrative: Buyer stated explicit ranking criteria on 06 May: vendors ranked on 'cost information, implementation timeline ... whether they have the capabilities', then the top two go to 'technical review, detailed timeline, detailed cost' before a decision. Functional criteria: SAP SRM replacement, high-volume invoice 3-way matching, internal/external e-catalogs feeding SAP via EDI, simplified PR release strategy, spend analytics, ease of use. The 10 Jun technical deep dive added integration/InfoSec criteria (SAP CI/BTP adapters, ISO 27001, SOC 2 Type 2). Confirmed, not inferred.

ai_positioning_strength.summary: Zycus's AI story has landed well: SABIC showed early enthusiasm for agentic vs rule-based systems (09 Dec 2025) and the 21 May demo featured Merlin Intake and autonomous sourcing. The next step is to tie the agentic narrative to a quantified ROI for the economic buyer.

items.quote: We would be looking for something to replace SRM ... we're looking to make it as simple, straightforward for the end user.
items.criterion: Replace end-of-life SAP SRM with simpler end-user experience

items.quote: We don't wanna go to a manual invoicing process ... invoicing is a must.
Zycus will take care of 80% of the activities from an integration standpoint, reducing the workload on your IT team.
items.criterion: SAP integration with low IT burden

items.quote: There is a concern about the potential need for existing vendors to undergo a new onboarding process ... similar IT-related issues experienced during the transition to Ariba, where all vendors had to be re-registered and the process remains incomplete after two years.
We'll have cost information, implementation timeline ... rank them accordingly.

customer_expectations_fit.summary: Strong functional and technical fit against SABIC's stated criteria, with the main exposure being commercial/ROI quantification and integration migration fear, not capability.

hard.ais_why:
• The Merlin product line is empty and Products does not include Merlin, Agentic AI or ANA - only iContract, iSource, iSupplier, eProcurement and eInvoicing. The description states bluntly: "informal process - no RFP - demos and decision. No strong compelling event". The buyer has not made AI a selection criterion.
• The May 6 2026 discovery call (31 minutes) with Klimack, Harris and Pacheco captured pain points centered on convoluted SAP processes, SAP SRM end-of-life, ease of use, EDI catalog integration and three-way invoice match. Must-haves: invoicing, e-catalogs and SRM replacement. None of the buyer quotes reference AI, Merlin, agentic or autonomous capability.
• AI importance is rated 'Important', but that is a Zycus-side field with no supporting buyer evidence. The actual product mix sold and the pain points discussed contain zero AI signals. Sourcing and CLM are only "nice-to-have" per the buyer - the narrow P2P scope further dampens any AI surface. This is Resistant.

Joe (procurement lead) is driving evaluation but has not introduced Zycus to budget owner. For a $518k P2P investment serving a multi-country carve-out entity, an EB (likely VP Procurement or CFO for Americas) must approve. Budgetary pricing was shared 11 Jun, but no EB meeting, no CFO presentation, no written approval visible. This is the single largest risk to advancing beyond Shortlisted/Vendor Selected and to forecast defensibility (Best Case with no EB is indefensible). If a competitor has EB/CFO sponsorship and Zycus does not, Zycus may lose on relationship/advocacy even with superior technical fit.

deltas.subject: Budgetary pricing shared 11 Jun, but no formal commercial proposal, ROI quantification, or CFO-ready business case delivered yet. Joe stated cost is a key ranking criterion (06 May: 'rank top 5 on cost, implementation timeline, capabilities'), but Zycus has not yet positioned the value story in ROI terms (cycle-time reduction, manual-process elimination, SRM replacement savings) to justify the $518k investment vs lower-cost or partial-scope alternatives. If a competitor undercuts Zycus on price or delivers a stronger ROI case before Zycus does, pricing could become a vulnerability. The next move is to deliver the ROI workshop within 14 days to quantify value and anchor the business case before vendor shortlisting (top 5 to top 2).

The buyer stated they will 'move pretty aggressively' (Joe 06 May), but no formal project timeline, go-live target date, or vendor selection deadline has been disclosed. deltas.subject: Single-thread risk: Joe Fischeco is the primary contact and champion-in-development, but he has not introduced Zycus to other key decision-makers (EB, CFO, VP Procurement) or demonstrated advocacy beyond process management. Hassan and Steven are engaged technical validators but influencers without commercial authority. Legal contacts (Brittany, Brandon) appeared for the first time on CLM demo 17 Jun, and their sentiment/engagement level is unknown. This is the single largest risk to advancing beyond Shortlisted/Vendor Selected and to forecast defensibility (Best Case with no EB is indefensible). Next move: request EB introduction through Joe ('Joe, we'd like to present the business case directly to the budget owner approving this investment; can you introduce us to the VP Procurement or CFO?'), and position the ROI workshop as the vehicle to earn EB sponsorship. Deliver the workshop within next 14 days to Joe + EB if possible, quantifying cycle-time reduction, manual-process elimination, and SRM replacement savings to build the CFO-ready business case Joe can champion internally.

Competitive shortlist undisclosed, leaving Zycus blind to peer threats: SABIC is running a formal top-5 vendor evaluation (Joe 06 May: 'rank top 5 on cost, implementation timeline, capabilities... probably talk further with the top two'), but no competitors except partial-scope incumbents (third-party orchestration platform pilot, Ariba being phased out, SRM being replaced) have been named on calls or in Salesforce. The buyer has been disciplined about not disclosing the shortlist, which is standard for RFP, but leaves Zycus unable to differentiate proactively. If the top 5 includes a well-funded direct P2P competitor (Coupa, Jaggaer, GEP) with stronger EB relationships or lower pricing, Zycus may lose on commercial or relationship factors not yet visible. Next move: extract competitive intel from Joe in next check-in (within 7 days): 'Joe, as you refine your top 5 ranking on cost, timeline, and capabilities, can you share how we compare and who else you're evaluating? We'd like to position our differentiation clearly—full Source-to-Pay suite vs partial-scope, SAP-certified integration, AI/Merlin cycle-time reduction—against your other options.' Use the ROI workshop to quantify Zycus's value vs partial-scope competitors (e.g., third-party pilot lacks invoicing/P2P per Alia 09 Dec 2025) and to anchor the business case before top-5-to-top-2 vendor selection occurs.

Champion is developing but not yet advocating; no power-level access: Joe Fischeco (procurement lead) is engaged and process-driving but has not demonstrated advocacy (no EB introduction, no internal docs shared, no competitive intel, no lobbying for Zycus with his leadership). Hassan Sheraz and Steven Kleinmak are aligned technical validators but influencers without commercial authority or EB access. No Economic Buyer, CFO, or C-level sponsor has been introduced or engaged, so Zycus has no executive champion or power-level relationship. For a $518k investment in a formal top-5 vendor evaluation, this is a high-risk single-thread: if a competitor has stronger EB/CFO sponsorship, Zycus may lose on relationship even with superior technical fit. Next move: convert Joe from engaged buyer to advocate by delivering a high-impact ROI workshop (within 14 days) that Joe can take to his EB/CFO, and request an EB introduction before vendor shortlisting occurs ('Joe, we'd like to present the business case directly to the executive approving this investment; can you arrange a 30-minute session with your VP Procurement or CFO?'). Use the workshop to quantify operational savings (manual sourcing elimination, SRM replacement, cycle-time reduction per Alia's AI interest 09 Dec 2025) and to position Zycus as the strategic partner for the carve-out entity, building Joe's confidence to advocate internally and establishing executive-level access.
